title_en: Primary and Secondary Heirs: Their Shares
title_id: Ahli Waris Utama dan Sekunder: Bagian-Bagian Mereka
content_en:
Islamic inheritance law employs a sophisticated system for categorizing heirs and determining their shares that has been elaborated by scholars over fourteen centuries into one of the most technically refined disciplines in all of Islamic jurisprudence. Understanding the basic framework of this system is essential for any Muslim who wishes to distribute their estate in accordance with divine guidance or to understand their own rights in the estate of a deceased family member.

The heirs in Islamic inheritance law are divided into several categories based on the source of their entitlement. The first and most important category consists of the Quranic heirs (ashab al-fara'id) — those whose specific fractional shares are designated in the Quran. These include: the husband (who receives one-half of his wife's estate if she had no children, or one-quarter if she had children), the wife (who receives one-quarter of her husband's estate if he had no children, or one-eighth if he had children), the daughter (who receives one-half if sole, or two-thirds collectively if multiple daughters with no son), the father (who receives one-sixth as a fixed share if the deceased had children, but becomes a residuary heir if the deceased had no children), the mother (who receives one-third in the absence of children, or one-sixth in their presence), and full sisters (under specific conditions).

The second major category consists of the asabat — the residuary heirs, who receive whatever remains of the estate after the Quranic heirs have received their fixed shares. The primary residuary heir is the deceased's son, who takes the entirety of the residue (or receives double the share of any daughter alongside him). The son's descendants, the father and his ascendants, and various other male agnatic relatives form the broader circle of residuary heirs, arranged in a precise order of priority.

The third category includes heirs related to the deceased through female links only (dhawul-arham) — such as the daughter's children, the mother's siblings, and others — who inherit only in the absence of both Quranic heirs and residuary heirs.

The principle of hajb — blocking or exclusion — governs the relationship between different categories of heirs: a closer heir blocks a more distant one from inheriting. For example, a son blocks the grandson from inheriting; the father blocks the grandfather; the full brother blocks the half-brother from the father's side. This principle, which operates with considerable complexity in actual estates, ensures that the closest relatives of the deceased take priority over more distant ones.

Siddiqi provides worked examples throughout his analysis to illustrate how these rules apply in practice. Consider a man who dies leaving a wife, a son, and a daughter: the wife receives one-eighth (her Quranic share when children are present), and the remaining seven-eighths are divided among the son and daughter in the ratio of two to one — so the son receives twice the daughter's share of the residue. Or consider a woman who dies leaving a husband and two daughters: the husband receives one-quarter (his share when children are present), the two daughters collectively receive two-thirds, and the remaining portion (one-quarter minus two-thirds, which is negative in simple addition) is addressed through the mechanism of 'awl — proportional reduction — which will be explained in the subsequent chapter.

The shares designated for women in the Islamic inheritance system — which are typically half those of men in the same category — require contextual understanding to appreciate their wisdom and justice. In the Islamic financial system, women have no mandatory financial obligations: they are not required to support spouses, children, parents, or other relatives from their personal wealth. Their inheritance — however smaller than the corresponding male share — is entirely their own. The male heir who receives a double share is simultaneously burdened with the obligation to maintain his wife and children from his own estate. When the full picture of Islamic financial obligations and entitlements is considered together, the wisdom of the designated shares becomes apparent.
content_id:
Hukum waris Islam menggunakan sistem yang canggih untuk mengkategorikan ahli waris dan menentukan bagian-bagian mereka yang telah diuraikan oleh para ulama selama empat belas abad menjadi salah satu disiplin ilmu yang paling halus secara teknis dalam seluruh fikih Islam. Memahami kerangka dasar sistem ini sangat penting bagi setiap Muslim yang ingin mendistribusikan hartanya sesuai dengan petunjuk ilahi atau untuk memahami hak-hak mereka sendiri dalam harta seorang anggota keluarga yang meninggal.

Para ahli waris dalam hukum waris Islam dibagi menjadi beberapa kategori berdasarkan sumber hak mereka. Kategori pertama dan terpenting terdiri dari ahli waris Al-Quran (ashab al-faraid) — mereka yang bagian pecahan khususnya ditetapkan dalam Al-Quran. Ini mencakup: suami (yang menerima setengah dari harta istrinya jika istri tidak memiliki anak, atau seperempat jika istri memiliki anak), istri (yang menerima seperempat dari harta suaminya jika suami tidak memiliki anak, atau seperdelapan jika suami memiliki anak), anak perempuan (yang menerima setengah jika satu-satunya, atau dua pertiga secara kolektif jika beberapa anak perempuan tanpa anak laki-laki), ayah (yang menerima seperenam sebagai bagian tetap jika almarhum memiliki anak, tetapi menjadi ahli waris asabah jika almarhum tidak memiliki anak), ibu (yang menerima sepertiga jika tidak ada anak, atau seperenam jika ada anak), dan saudara perempuan kandung (dalam kondisi-kondisi tertentu).

Kategori utama kedua terdiri dari asabat — ahli waris asabah, yang menerima apa pun yang tersisa dari harta setelah ahli waris Al-Quran menerima bagian tetap mereka. Ahli waris asabah utama adalah anak laki-laki almarhum, yang mengambil seluruh sisa (atau menerima dua kali bagian anak perempuan mana pun yang ada bersamanya). Keturunan anak laki-laki, ayah dan leluhurnya, serta berbagai kerabat agnati laki-laki lainnya membentuk lingkaran ahli waris asabah yang lebih luas, diatur dalam urutan prioritas yang tepat.

Kategori ketiga mencakup ahli waris yang berhubungan dengan almarhum hanya melalui hubungan perempuan (dzawul-arham) — seperti anak-anak dari anak perempuan, saudara kandung ibu, dan lainnya — yang hanya mewarisi jika tidak ada ahli waris Al-Quran maupun ahli waris asabah.

Prinsip hajb — pemblokiran atau penghalangan — mengatur hubungan antara kategori-kategori ahli waris yang berbeda: ahli waris yang lebih dekat menghalangi ahli waris yang lebih jauh untuk mewarisi. Misalnya, seorang anak laki-laki menghalangi cucu laki-laki dari mewarisi; ayah menghalangi kakek; saudara laki-laki kandung menghalangi saudara laki-laki seayah. Prinsip ini, yang beroperasi dengan kompleksitas yang cukup besar dalam harta yang sebenarnya, memastikan bahwa kerabat terdekat dari almarhum diprioritaskan di atas yang lebih jauh.

Siddiqi memberikan contoh-contoh yang dikerjakan sepanjang analisisnya untuk mengilustrasikan bagaimana aturan-aturan ini berlaku dalam praktik. Pertimbangkan seorang laki-laki yang meninggal meninggalkan istri, seorang anak laki-laki, dan seorang anak perempuan: istri menerima seperdelapan (bagian Al-Qurannya saat ada anak), dan tujuh delapan sisanya dibagi antara anak laki-laki dan anak perempuan dengan rasio dua berbanding satu — sehingga anak laki-laki menerima dua kali bagian anak perempuan dari sisa. Atau pertimbangkan seorang perempuan yang meninggal meninggalkan suami dan dua anak perempuan: suami menerima seperempat (bagiannya saat ada anak), dua anak perempuan secara kolektif menerima dua pertiga, dan bagian yang tersisa (seperempat dikurangi dua pertiga, yang negatif dalam penjumlahan sederhana) ditangani melalui mekanisme 'awl — pengurangan proporsional — yang akan dijelaskan pada bab berikutnya.

Bagian-bagian yang ditetapkan bagi perempuan dalam sistem waris Islam — yang biasanya setengah dari bagian laki-laki dalam kategori yang sama — memerlukan pemahaman kontekstual untuk menghargai kebijaksanaan dan keadilannya. Dalam sistem keuangan Islam, perempuan tidak memiliki kewajiban finansial wajib: mereka tidak diwajibkan untuk menafkahi pasangan, anak-anak, orang tua, atau kerabat lainnya dari kekayaan pribadi mereka. Warisan mereka — betapapun lebih kecil dari bagian laki-laki yang sesuai — sepenuhnya milik mereka sendiri. Ahli waris laki-laki yang menerima bagian ganda secara bersamaan dibebani dengan kewajiban untuk menafkahi istri dan anak-anaknya dari hartanya sendiri. Ketika gambaran lengkap kewajiban dan hak finansial Islam dipertimbangkan bersama-sama, kebijaksanaan dari bagian-bagian yang telah ditetapkan menjadi jelas.
title_ar: Primary and Secondary Heirs: Their Shares
content_ar: الإسلامي الميراث law employs a sophisticated system for categorizing heirs and determining their shares that has been elaborated by العلماء over fourteen centuries into one of the most technically refined disciplines in all of الإسلامي الفقه الإسلامي. Understanding the basic framework of this system is essential for any المسلم who wishes to distribute their estate in accordance with divine guidance or to understand their own rights in the estate of a deceased family member. The heirs in الإسلامي الميراث law are divided into several categories based on the source of their entitlement. The first and most important category consists of the Quranic heirs (ashab al-fara'id) — those whose specific fractional shares are designated in القرآن الكريم. These include: the husband (who receives one-half of his wife's estate if she had no children, or one-quarter if she had children), the wife (who receives one-quarter of her husband's estate if he had no children, or one-eighth if he had children), the daughter (who receives one-half if sole, or two-thirds collectively if multiple daughters with no son), the father (who receives one-sixth as a fixed share if the deceased had children, but becomes a residuary heir if the deceased had no children), the mother (who receives one-third in the absence of children, or one-sixth in their presence), and full sisters (under specific conditions). The second major category consists of the asabat — the residuary heirs, who receive whatever remains of the estate after the Quranic heirs have received their fixed shares. The primary residuary heir is the deceased's son, who takes the entirety of the residue (or receives double the share of any daughter alongside him). The son's descendants, the father and his ascendants, and various other male agnatic relatives form the broader circle of residuary heirs, arranged in a precise order of priority. The third category includes heirs related to the deceased through female links only (dhawul-arham) — such as the daughter's children, the mother's siblings, and others — who inherit only in the absence of both Quranic heirs and residuary heirs. The principle of hajb — blocking or exclusion — governs the relationship between different categories of heirs: a closer heir blocks a more distant one from inheriting. For example, a son blocks the grandson from inheriting; the father blocks the grandfather; the full brother blocks the half-brother from the father's side. This principle, which operates with considerable complexity in actual estates, ensures that the closest relatives of the deceased take priority over more distant ones. Siddiqi provides worked examples throughout his analysis to illustrate how these rules apply in practice. Consider a man who dies leaving a wife, a son, and a daughter: the wife receives one-eighth (her Quranic share when children are present), and the remaining seven-eighths are divided among the son and daughter in the ratio of two to one — so the son receives twice the daughter's share of the residue. Or consider a woman who dies leaving a husband and two daughters: the husband receives one-quarter (his share when children are present), the two daughters collectively receive two-thirds, and the remaining portion (one-quarter minus two-thirds, which is negative in simple addition) is addressed through the mechanism of 'awl — proportional reduction — which will be explained in the subsequent chapter. The shares designated for women in the الإسلامي الميراث system — which are typically half those of men in the same category — require contextual understanding to appreciate their wisdom and justice. In the الإسلامي financial system, women have no واجب financial obligations: they are not required to support spouses, children, parents, or other relatives from their personal wealth. Their الميراث — however smaller than the corresponding male share — is entirely their own. The male heir who receives a double share is simultaneously burdened with the obligation to maintain his wife and children from his own estate. When the full picture of الإسلامي financial obligations and entitlements is considered together, the wisdom of the designated shares becomes apparent.
